title: Sinusoids
summary: This page explains how to sketch sinusoidal signals by interpreting phase shifts as time delays. It demonstrates that delaying C cos(ω₀t) by 60° yields C cos(ω₀t − 60°), and that a quarter-cycle delay of 90° transforms cosine into sine, verifying the trigonometric identity C cos(ω₀t − π/2) = C sin(ω₀t).

figures.caption: Sketching a sinusoid, showing (a) C cos ω₀t, (b) C sin ω₀t, and (c) the phase-shifted sinusoid C cos(ω₀t − 60°) obtained by delaying C cos ω₀t by one-sixth of a period (T₀/6).
figures.description: The figure has three time-domain sinusoid plots with horizontal axis t and marked times -T0, -T0/2, 0, T0/2, and T0; amplitude is labeled C, and the period relation T0 = 1/f0 = 2π/ω0 is shown. Panel (a) plots C cos(ω0 t), panel (b) plots C sin(ω0 t), and panel (c) overlays C cos(ω0 t) with the shifted waveform C cos(ω0 t - 60°). Students should notice the different starting values of cosine and sine at t = 0, the common amplitude and period, and that a 60° phase delay corresponds to shifting the cosine right by T0/6.
